Sources: [SRH WG Bulletin Q1 2023](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/srh_working_group_bulletin_quarter_i_2023_2.pdf); [SRH WG Bulletin Q3 2023](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/srh_wg_bulletin_quarter_iii_2023_final.pdf); [Health Sector Bulletin Dec 2023](https://rohingyaresponse.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/CXB-Health-Sector-Bulletin-Dec-2023-1.pdf); [Health Sector Bulletin Aug 2022](https://reliefweb.int/attachments/2f6538fb-4542-4e83-a285-13c3c0174db8/final_health_sector_bulletin_august_2022.pdf); [UNHCR HIS Mae La 2018](https://his.unhcr.org/ar2018/files/TH/Thailand_2018_Maela.pdf); [Cambridge Core](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/primary-health-care-research-and-development/article/mother-child-and-adolescent-health-outcomes-in-two-longterm-refugee-camp-settings-at-the-thaimyanmar-border-20002018-a-retrospective-analysis/5A2A03E5B33226D61F84A028BB229992)

**Interpretation:** Reporting inconsistency in Cox's Bazar limits direct comparison. The 17% figure from August 2022 is difficult to reconcile with the 85% reported five months later in Q1 2023, suggesting changes in measurement methodology rather than dramatic improvement. The Health Sector August 2022 bulletin explicitly states "ANC4 coverage is currently at 17% only." By contrast, the Karen camp system has consistently recorded >90% ANC coverage for over a decade, reflecting stable measurement rather than fluctuation. A critical quality gap identified in the Cox's Bazar MPMSR audits is that high ANC attendance did not reliably translate into facility-based delivery — in 2022, maternal death reviews found incomplete history-taking, multiple ANC cards, and weak counseling when influential family members were absent ([MPMSR 2022](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/mpmsr_annual_report_2022.pdf.pdf)).

### 1.3 Skilled Birth Attendance and Facility-Based Deliveries

| Location | Period | SBA / Facility Delivery Rate | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rohingya, Cox's Bazar | 2017–18 | ~22% facility births; 88% home deliveries | Health Sector / CHW reports |
| Rohingya, Cox's Bazar | Jun 2021 | 67% facility-based | WHO Health Cluster Bulletin |
| Rohingya, Cox's Bazar | Aug 2022 | 75% facility births | Health Sector Bulletin Aug 2022 |
| Rohingya, Cox's Bazar | 2023 | 82% overall facility births in camps | SRH WG Bulletin Q3 2023 |
| Rohingya, Cox's Bazar | Dec 2023 | 85% health facility births | Health Sector Bulletin Dec 2023 |
| Karen, Mae La camp | 2018 | 95% births attended by skilled personnel | UNHCR HIS 2018 |
| Karen, MRML/MLO camps | 2000–2018 | ~80% SBA | Cambridge 2024 |

Sources: [WHO Health Cluster Bulletin Apr–Jun 2021](https://healthcluster.who.int/docs/librariesprovider16/meeting-reports/bangladesh-health-sector-bulletin-apr-june-2021.pdf?download=true&sfvrsn=cef9a240_5); [Health Sector Bulletin Aug 2022](https://reliefweb.int/attachments/2f6538fb-4542-4e83-a285-13c3c0174db8/final_health_sector_bulletin_august_2022.pdf); [SRH WG Bulletin Q3 2023](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/srh_wg_bulletin_quarter_iii_2023_final.pdf); [Health Sector Bulletin Dec 2023](https://rohingyaresponse.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/CXB-Health-Sector-Bulletin-Dec-2023-1.pdf); [UNHCR HIS Mae La 2018](https://his.unhcr.org/ar2018/files/TH/Thailand_2018_Maela.pdf)

**Interpretation:** The Rohingya response achieved one of the most dramatic SBA improvements documented in any humanitarian setting — from approximately 22% facility births in 2018 to 82–85% by 2023. This reflects the rapid deployment of midwife-led birthing centres (35 MLBCs across the camps), CHW outreach achieving >85% weekly household coverage, and a shift in community norms. Nevertheless, it remains below Mae La's 95% SBA, and — critically — the Cox's Bazar gains in facility delivery have not been matched by reductions in mortality, as discussed below.

### 1.4 Postpartum Hemorrhage Burden

Directly comparable PPH incidence rates per 1,000 births are not available for either setting in the gathered evidence. No source provides a population-based PPH incidence numerator and denominator for either Cox's Bazar or Mae La for the 2017–2023 period. Consequently, the comparison below relies on mortality attribution as an indirect measure of PPH clinical significance.

**Cox's Bazar — cause-specific maternal death data:**

| Period | Obstetric haemorrhage as share of maternal deaths | PPH-specific share |
|---|---|---|
| 2017–18 RAMOS | 28% of pregnancy-related deaths | 61% of haemorrhage deaths = ~17.1% of all PRDs |
| 2021 MPMSR | 29.2% of 96 maternal deaths | 50% of haemorrhage = ~14.6% of all deaths |
| 2022 MPMSR | 44% of 83 maternal deaths (27.7% when recalculated from table: 23 direct haemorrhage deaths / 83) | Not disaggregated in gathered text |
| 2023 MPMSR | 35.1% (20 haemorrhage deaths / 57) | Not disaggregated |
| 2024 MPMSR | 48% of maternal deaths | 22% PPH specifically, 26% APH |

Sources: [RAMOS Summary](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/Final%20RAMOS%20Summary%20Report%20for%20circulation_0.pdf); [MPMSR 2021](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/mpmsr_annual_report_2021.pdf); [MPMSR 2022](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/mpmsr_annual_report_2022.pdf.pdf); [MPMSR 2023](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/2024-09/MPMSR%20Annual%20Report%202023%20Final.docx.pdf); [MPMSR 2024](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/2025-08/MPMSR%20Annual%20Report%202024%20Final.docx.pdf)

A concerning trend emerges: obstetric haemorrhage as a proportion of maternal deaths in Cox's Bazar has increased from 28–29% (2017–2021) to 44–48% (2022–2024). This suggests that while ANC coverage and facility delivery rates improved, the quality of emergency obstetric care — specifically active management of the third stage of labour, timely recognition of PPH, and capacity for urgent intervention — has not kept pace.

**Mae La / Thai border:** The Karen camps have such low absolute numbers of maternal deaths (10 deaths across 14 years in MRML/MLO; 0 in Mae La 2018) that robust cause-specific PPH mortality fractions cannot be calculated. An SMRU intrapartum quality assessment noted that PPH was the most common observed complication, and while SBAs followed the PPH protocol, they were sometimes slow to recognize severity and take action ([BMC Pregnancy and Childbirth 2015](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12884-015-0444-0)). The near-absence of maternal deaths despite this suggests that the SMRU referral system — with on-site BEmONC and CEmONC at Thai hospitals 30–75 minutes away — effectively prevents PPH from progressing to mortality. In Cox's Bazar, the combination of variable BEmONC signal-function availability (only 62% of Ukhia facilities providing all six functions), inconsistent blood transfusion access, and referral delays creates a lethality gap for PPH that Mae La's system largely closes.

### 1.5 Neonatal Mortality Within 28 Days

| Location | Period | NMR (per 1,000 live births) | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rohingya, Cox's Bazar | Sep 2017–Dec 2018 | 27.0 (95% CI 22.3–31.8) | BMJ Global Health (Amsalu et al., 2022) |
| Rohingya, Cox's Bazar | 2017–18 (RAMOS) | 18.0 (531 neonatal deaths) | UNFPA RAMOS Summary |
| Rohingya, Cox's Bazar | Jan–Aug 2022 | ~6.7 (134 reported deaths; likely under-ascertained) | Health Sector Bulletin Aug 2022 |
| Rohingya, Cox's Bazar | Week 38, 2023 | ~9.8 (215 reported deaths; reported-death NMR only) | SRH WG Bulletin Q3 2023 |
| Karen, Mae La camp | 2018 | 6.0 | UNHCR HIS Site Report 2018 |
| Karen, Mae La camp | 2011 | 10.7 (after SCBU introduction) | PLOS ONE (Turner et al., 2013) |
| Karen, Mae La camp | 2009 | 21.8 (pre-SCBU) | PLOS ONE (Turner et al., 2013) |
| Karen, MRML/MLO | 2018 | ~7.7 | Cambridge Primary Health Care R&D |

Sources: [BMJ Global Health — NMR Rohingya](https://gh.bmj.com/content/bmjgh/7/4/e008110.full.pdf); [RAMOS Summary](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/Final%20RAMOS%20Summary%20Report%20for%20circulation_0.pdf); [Health Sector Bulletin Aug 2022](https://reliefweb.int/attachments/2f6538fb-4542-4e83-a285-13c3c0174db8/final_health_sector_bulletin_august_2022.pdf); [SRH WG Bulletin Q3 2023](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/srh_wg_bulletin_quarter_iii_2023_final.pdf); [UNHCR HIS Mae La 2018](https://his.unhcr.org/ar2018/files/TH/Thailand_2018_Maela.pdf); [PLOS ONE — SCBU Mae La](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article/file?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0072721&type=printable)

**Interpretation:** The most robust comparator is the 2017–18 prospective surveillance NMR of 27.0 per 1,000 in Cox's Bazar versus Mae La's 2018 NMR of 6.0 per 1,000 — a 4.5-fold difference. The lower RAMOS-derived figure of 18.0 likely reflects different surveillance methods (retrospective vs. prospective). The later Cox's Bazar reported-death figures of 6.7–9.8 per 1,000 represent facility- and community-reported deaths only, not a surveillance-adjusted rate, and almost certainly understate true mortality given that 76.3% of neonatal deaths in the 2017–18 prospective study occurred at home or in the community. The Mae La NMR of 6.0 — achieved through a combination of high SBA coverage, BEmONC capability on-site, SCBU care for low-birthweight neonates, and Thai hospital referral — is comparable to Thai national levels.

---

## 2. Healthcare Delivery Models

### 2.1 Cox's Bazar: Multi-Agency NGO-Operated Field Hospitals

The Cox's Bazar maternal health system is delivered through a humanitarian coordination architecture involving UN agencies, international and national NGOs, and field hospitals. As of 2023, the network comprised approximately 74 health facilities including 35 Midwife-Led Birthing Centres (MLBCs), 77 health posts, 45 primary healthcare centres, three CEmONC facilities, and eight secondary-care facilities, all coordinated through the Health Sector (led by WHO) and the SRH Working Group (led by UNFPA) ([SRH WG Bulletin Q1 2023](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/srh_working_group_bulletin_quarter_i_2023_2.pdf)).

The MLBC model — by design — deploys teams of 4–6 midwives per centre, providing 24/7 midwifery-led care with a medical doctor available for consultation and referral to NGO-run higher-level facilities in Cox's Bazar. UNFPA supported approximately 260–466 midwives across the camps during the study period, alongside the Emergency Referral Transport System (ERTS) operating eight ambulances 24/7 across all 34 camps. The CHW network comprised over 1,400 community health workers achieving >85% weekly household coverage for health messaging ([Health Sector Bulletin Q1 2022](https://reliefweb.int/attachments/bb1223e6-9d2f-3020-92b3-8290bdae4e5d/health_sector_bulletin_18_q1_jan-mar_2022.pdf)).

**Strengths:** Rapid scale-up from an emergency baseline; dramatic increase in facility delivery rates; strong CHW outreach; improved ANC coverage.

**Weaknesses identified in MPMSR audits:** Fragmentation across 77+ implementing partners; 24/7 CEmONC access difficult due to staff shortages; first delay (delay in seeking care) most significant, driven by preference for informal healthcare providers and poor knowledge of danger signs; second-delay factors including transport unavailability in 10 cases and infrastructure inaccessibility; third-delay factors including inadequate blood transfusion access, provider skill gaps, laboratory-service gaps, and non-compliance with referral pathways ([MPMSR 2021](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/mpmsr_annual_report_2021.pdf); [MPMSR 2023](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/2024-09/MPMSR%20Annual%20Report%202023%20Final.docx.pdf)).

### 2.2 Mae La Camp: SMRU Midwife-Led Unit with Thai MoPH Integration

The Karen camps operate under a model developed over nearly 40 years of institutional continuity. The Shoklo Malaria Research Unit (SMRU), a field unit of Oxford University's Mahidol-Oxford Tropical Medicine Research Unit, has been the sole provider of antenatal care and delivery services in Mae La since 1997. SMRU's maternity unit provides all seven BEmONC signal functions (parenteral oxytocics, antibiotics, anticonvulsants, manual removal of placenta, removal of retained products, assisted vaginal delivery, and neonatal resuscitation) with 24-hour telephone backup from expatriate obstetricians ([PLOS ONE — SBA training](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0164363)).

Integration with the Thai Ministry of Public Health operates through multiple channels: vaccines are provided free by the Thai MoPH following the national EPI policy; the Bureau of Epidemiology at the Thai MoPH receives surveillance reports directly; birth certificates are supported by the Royal Thai Government; and all caesarean sections and complicated deliveries are referred to Thai public hospitals 30–75 minutes away by car. This is not "full integration" in the sense of refugees accessing ordinary Thai primary care, but rather a structured camp-based NGO model with formalised referral, surveillance, and vaccination linkages to the Thai state system ([Cambridge Core 2024](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/primary-health-care-research-and-development/article/mother-child-and-adolescent-health-outcomes-in-two-longterm-refugee-camp-settings-at-the-thaimyanmar-border-20002018-a-retrospective-analysis/5A2A03E5B33226D61F84A028BB229992); [EID/CDC Thailand camps surveillance](https://wwwnc.cdc.gov/eid/article/28/13/pdfs/22-0324-combined.pdf)).

**Strengths:** Stable, long-running maternal-care model; locally trained SBAs drawn from refugee communities; high ANC coverage and near-universal SBA; referral to Thai hospitals within two hours for CEmONC-level care; proven capacity to reduce NMR through SCBU intervention.

**Weaknesses:** Dependence on NGO/donor financing (though diversified across research grants, charitable donations, and DG ECHO); not a fully integrated public entitlement model for refugees; populations in more remote southern camps (MRML/MLO) face 3.5–7 hour referral times to Mae Sariang Hospital.

---

## 3. Structural Correlates: Midwife Ratios, EmONC Access, and Cultural Mediation

### 3.1 Midwife-to-Population Ratios

| Factor | Cox's Bazar (Rohingya) | Mae La / Thai Border (Karen) |
|---|---|---|
| Midwife count | ~466 midwives across 35 MLBCs (2023); ~260 across facilities/WFS (2022 UNFPA) | ~26 midwives + 23 MCH assistants for ~20,000 (MRML/MLO); Mae La: 72 qualified nurses per UNHCR HIS (midwives not separately enumerated) |
| Population served | ~900,000 refugees | ~37,000–40,000 (Mae La) |
| Approximate midwife:population | ~0.52 midwives per 1,000 population | ~1.3 midwives per 1,000 (MRML/MLO) |
| SBA-to-population ratio | ~1,055–1,245 SBAs ≈ 1.2–1.4 per 1,000 (April 2022: 51% of Sphere standard of 23/10,000) | SMRU-trained SBAs staff the delivery unit; no published SBA:population ratio identified |
| Minimum staffing met | Only 54% of PHCCs met minimum midwife staffing (2024 Joint Monitoring) | Consistent full staffing through institutional model |

Sources: [Health Sector Bulletin Dec 2023](https://rohingyaresponse.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/CXB-Health-Sector-Bulletin-Dec-2023-1.pdf); [UNFPA SitRep Jul–Sep 2022](https://reliefweb.int/attachments/ee9656d4-1d92-40f4-a10e-9a3407c43ed6/external_sitrep_july_-sep_2022_unfpa_coxs_bazar_comms.pdf); [Health Sector Strategic Plan 2023–2024](https://rohingyaresponse.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/Health-Sector-Strategic-Plan-2023-2024.pdf); [Cambridge Core 2024](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/primary-health-care-research-and-development/article/mother-child-and-adolescent-health-outcomes-in-two-longterm-refugee-camp-settings-at-the-thaimyanmar-border-20002018-a-retrospective-analysis/5A2A03E5B33226D61F84A028BB229992); [UNHCR HIS Mae La 2018](https://his.unhcr.org/ar2018/files/TH/Thailand_2018_Maela.pdf)

### 3.2 Emergency Obstetric Care Within 2-Hour Transport Radius

**Cox's Bazar:** The formal architecture shows three CEmONC facilities, eight secondary-care facilities, and eight 24/7 ambulances covering all 34 camps. However, operational evidence shows this theoretical coverage frequently failed in practice. MPMSR 2021 documented second-delay factors in 10 of 96 maternal deaths where transport was unavailable, and four additional cases where infrastructure was inaccessible. The 2022 MPMSR listed "transport crisis" as a delay-two factor. By August 2023, the closure of the Hope Field Hospital removed the principal CEmONC facility from the camp network entirely, leaving a gap affecting 10 camps. The SRH Joint Monitoring found that no Health Post had an effective blood transfusion mechanism, and only 16% ensured 24-hour postpartum observation ([SRH-QIAS 2025](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/2date26-date5/SRH%20Joint%20Monitoring%20Report_2025_SRH%20Quality%20Improvement%20and%20Accountability%20System_VApril27.docx.pdf)).

**Mae La:** SMRU clinics provide all seven BEmONC signal functions on-site. Women requiring caesarean section or comprehensive EmONC are transferred to Thai hospitals — Mae Sot Hospital is approximately 60 km (1 hour by car) from Mae La camp, well within the two-hour standard. SMRU funds hospital care in Thailand. The proportion of births requiring emergency secondary referral was approximately 7% in the MRML/MLO camps, indicating that on-site BEmONC successfully manages the majority of obstetric complications without transport ([Cambridge Core 2024](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/primary-health-care-research-and-development/article/mother-child-and-adolescent-health-outcomes-in-two-longterm-refugee-camp-settings-at-the-thaimyanmar-border-20002018-a-retrospective-analysis/5A2A03E5B33226D61F84A028BB229992)).

### 3.3 Cultural Mediation and Traditional Birth Attendants

The two settings have adopted fundamentally opposite strategies toward traditional birth attendants:

**Cox's Bazar:** TBAs are treated as competitors to be displaced by facility-based care. Community Health Workers conduct household visits to promote facility delivery, and TBAs are incentivised to act as doulas accompanying women to facilities rather than performing home births. This shift is credited with the increase in SBA from ~22% to ~85%. However, the 2021 MPMSR found that preference for informal healthcare providers was the single largest contributor to first delay, cited in 31.6% of reviewed maternal deaths. The 2024 MPMSR report documented that women arriving at facilities had experienced an average 19-hour delay due to "home trials attended by traditional birth attendants" ([MPMSR 2024](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/2025-08/MPMSR%20Annual%20Report%202024%20Final.docx.pdf)). Qualitative research identified cultural norms around female modesty, restricted mobility, and decision-making authority (with husbands, mothers-in-law, and majhis as gatekeepers) as persistent barriers to care-seeking ([BMJ 2021](https://www.bmj.com/content/375/bmj.n2722)).

**Mae La:** TBAs are *integrated* as support persons within facility-based care. In 55% of observed deliveries at SMRU's Mae La unit, the woman was accompanied by a TBA into the birth room; in 35%, a female relative; in 10%, the husband ([BMC Pregnancy and Childbirth 2015](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12884-015-0444-0)). Approximately 110 TBAs remained active in the two southern Karen camps alongside 26 midwives and 23 MCH assistants. The SMRU SBA training programme, formalised in 2010 and delivered over six months to local Karen and Burman women, created a pathway for community members to transition from lay birth attendance to credentialed skilled care ([PLOS ONE — SBA training](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0164363)). This model — welcoming TBAs as companions rather than excluding them — built community trust over decades and contributed to ≤5% home-birth rates in Mae La by 2018.

---

## 4. Impact of the 2021 UNFPA Funding Reduction

### 4.1 Global Context

In April 2021, the United Kingdom government reduced its expected contribution to UNFPA Supplies by approximately 85% — from £154 million (US$211 million) to approximately £23 million (US$32 million) — with an additional £12 million (US$17 million) cut from UNFPA core operating funds. UNFPA estimated that the withdrawn funding would otherwise have helped prevent approximately 250,000 maternal and child deaths, 14.6 million unintended pregnancies, and 4.3 million unsafe abortions globally ([UNFPA Statement](https://www.unfpa.org/press/statement-uk-government-funding-cuts)). The UK had been UNFPA's largest donor; the cuts created a global contraceptive commodity shortage and forced programme reductions across multiple country operations.

### 4.2 Cox's Bazar: Delayed but Severe Service Disruption

The impact on Cox's Bazar was not immediate but cascaded over 2021–2023:

**Short-term mitigation (2021):** In January 2021 — three months before the UK cuts were announced — UNFPA Bangladesh signed a US$38.8 million agreement with the Government of Bangladesh to implement the World Bank-financed Health and Gender Support Project, strengthening SRHR and GBV services across Cox's Bazar district including the Rohingya camps ([UNFPA Bangladesh](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/en/news/strengthening-health-and-gender-services-coxs-bazar-district-us388-million-through-world-bank)). This World Bank financing partially insulated the response from the immediate effects of the UK aid contraction.

**Structural vulnerability:** The Cox's Bazar maternal health system is almost entirely dependent on humanitarian/NGO funding. The MLBCs are NGO-staffed (e.g., RTM-International), the referral chain terminates in NGO-run hospitals within the camp/Cox's Bazar area, and UNFPA serves as both the lead coordinating agency and the principal funder for SRH services. There is no host-government budget line for refugee maternal health — a structural contrast to the Mae La model, where comprehensive EmONC terminates in the Thai public hospital system.

**The Hope Field Hospital closure (August 2023):** The most severe documented impact was the termination of UNFPA financial support for the 100-bed Hope Field Hospital for Women — the principal CEmONC facility in the camps — effective 1 August 2023. The hospital had performed 2,344 deliveries in 2022 (up from 802 in 2020), including over 1,100 caesarean sections, and its closure terminated approximately 300 staff positions and left a major CEmONC gap across 10 camps. The Cox's Bazar Civil Surgeon noted that "pregnant mothers in severe physical condition were referred to this hospital," and the gap was only partially addressed when CEmONC services were restored at Teknaf Health Complex in Q1 2024 ([Parbatta News](https://en.parbattanews.com/hope-field-hospital-closure-leaves-pregnant-rohingya-women-in-limbo/); [SRH WG Annual Bulletin 2022](https://bangladesh.unfpa.org/sites/default/files/pub-pdf/sexual_and_reproductive_health_working_annual_bulletin_2022.pdf)).

**Causal attribution caution:** While the temporal sequence is clear, a direct causal link between the 2021 UNFPA funding reduction and the Hope Hospital closure in 2023 is not explicitly made in the gathered sources. The SRH Working Group bulletins from 2021 describe continuity efforts despite COVID-19, flooding, and broader funding stress rather than a documented causal chain. The evidence is consistent with — but does not prove — the hypothesis that the UK cuts gradually eroded UNFPA's capacity to maintain the full scope of its Cox's Bazar SRH portfolio.

### 4.3 Mae La Camp: Relative Resilience Through Diversified Funding

The Thailand border camps were less directly dependent on UNFPA funding for core maternal health services:

- SMRU's maternal health services are funded through research grants (including Wellcome Trust), charitable donations, and institutional support from Oxford University — a funding base structurally separate from UNFPA country programme budgets.
- UNFPA supported the Planned Parenthood Association of Thailand (PPAT) to operate SRH clinics in Mae La, focusing on family planning counselling and peer education ([UNFPA — Thailand's refugee camps](https://www.unfpa.org/news/thailands-refugee-camps-shifting-attitudes-about-sexual-and-reproductive-health)). UK cuts to UNFPA Supplies would have constrained contraceptive commodity availability through this channel, but core obstetric services (ANC, delivery, PNC, BEmONC, referral) were insulated.
- DG ECHO funding for Malteser International supported the broader camp primary health infrastructure in the southern camps, again a diversified funding stream.

The gathered evidence does not document any quantified 2021 UNFPA funding-cut effect on Mae La maternal health indicators (ANC4, SBA, PPH, NMR, staffing, or service continuity). The Karen camp system's demonstrated resilience to earlier funding shocks — for example, when the Thai Department of Public Health stopped contraceptive supply after the 1997 Asian financial crisis and SMRU secured alternative funding — suggests institutional absorptive capacity for donor fluctuations that the Cox's Bazar system, as a newer and more monolithic humanitarian architecture, had not yet developed.

---

## 5. Synthesis and Interpretation of Divergence

The 4.5-fold difference in neonatal mortality and the approximately 2-fold difference in maternal mortality between the two populations — despite the Rohingya response achieving near-parity on ANC4+ coverage (75–85% vs. >90%) and rapidly closing the SBA gap (85% vs. 95%) — suggests that **intrapartum and immediate postnatal care quality, rather than coverage of antenatal contact or delivery attendance per se, is the discriminating factor.**

Three mechanisms plausibly explain the quality differential:

**1. Institutional continuity.** SMRU has provided uninterrupted maternal care in the Karen camps for nearly 40 years, with the same organisation collecting pregnancy outcome data, training staff, maintaining clinical protocols, and accountable for mortality. The Cox's Bazar system, by contrast, operates through 77+ implementing partners with variable clinical standards and frequent turnover. In 2022, only 56% of facility-based maternal deaths were reported through the formal event-based MPMSR system — a surveillance completeness gap that makes quality improvement difficult to target.

**2. Cultural mediation.** The SMRU strategy of welcoming TBAs as birth-room support persons has built trust in facility-based delivery without creating an adversarial relationship between formal healthcare providers and traditional birth attendants. The Rohingya response's strategy of displacing TBAs — while achieving impressive increases in facility delivery rates — has left a residual population of women who delay seeking care due to preference for a familiar birth attendant, documented as the single largest factor in first-delay deaths.

**3. Referral endpoint stability.** The Mae La model — BEmONC on-site, CEmONC at Thai public hospitals 30–75 minutes away, funded by the Thai state — provides a durable, rights-adjacent safety net that is not contingent on humanitarian funding cycles. The Cox's Bazar model — BEmONC of variable quality across facilities, CEmONC concentrated in 1–3 NGO-operated field hospitals with no host-government budget line — was demonstrated to be vulnerable when the Hope Field Hospital closed in August 2023, creating a CEmONC gap across 10 camps. The parallel humanitarian system, despite remarkable achievements in expanding coverage, cannot replicate the institutional permanence, quality assurance, and accountability of an integrated national health system.

---

## 6. Evidence Limitations

This report should be read with the following evidentiary constraints acknowledged:

- **Mae La 2019–2023 data gap:** No site-level UNHCR HIS maternal-health reports for Mae La for the 2019–2023 period were available in the gathered evidence. The Mae La indicators used are 2018 or earlier. The camp's population declined from ~40,000 to below 30,000 by 2022, and the 2021 Myanmar coup altered the border context, but the effect (if any) on Mae La maternal outcomes specifically cannot be documented from the evidence in hand.
- **PPH incidence:** Neither setting has a published population-based PPH incidence rate. Mortality attribution is the only available comparative metric, and mortality attribution is not incidence.
- **Cox's Bazar MMR trend:** The apparent increase from RAMOS 179 to MPMSR 295–321 is confounded by changes in surveillance methodology and cannot be confidently attributed to service deterioration versus improved detection.
- **Denominator uncertainty:** Cox's Bazar ANC4 and facility-delivery figures are reported inconsistently across Health Sector bulletins, with some presenting numerators only and others using varying denominator definitions. The 17% ANC4 figure in August 2022 and the 85% figure in Q1 2023 are difficult to reconcile under any plausible rate-of-change scenario, suggesting measurement changes rather than real improvement.

---

## 7. Conclusions

1. **Mae La camp's maternal health outcomes — a 2018 NMR of 6 per 1,000 and MMR of approximately 126 per 100,000 — are substantially better than those documented among Rohingya refugees in Cox's Bazar (NMR 27.0; MMR 295–321).** The Karen outcomes approach Thai national levels; the Rohingya outcomes remain at levels associated with acute emergencies despite seven years of humanitarian presence.

2. **The healthcare delivery models differ less as "NGO field hospitals versus Thai MOPH integration" and more as "large, fragmented, multi-actor emergency humanitarian system wholly dependent on donor funding" versus "small, institutionally stable, research-unit-led service with a durable referral endpoint in the Thai public hospital system."** The Mae La model's integration of locally trained refugee SBAs, culturally accommodated TBAs, on-site BEmONC, and Thai hospital CEmONC within a one-hour transport radius represents the more effective configuration.

3. **PPH incidence cannot be quantitatively compared** due to absence of population-based incidence data in both settings, but PPH as a cause of maternal death is rising in Cox's Bazar (from 28% of pregnancy-related deaths in 2017–18 to 48% of maternal deaths in 2024), indicating a widening gap in emergency obstetric care quality rather than convergence.

4. **The 2021 UNFPA funding reduction had a demonstrable structural impact on Cox's Bazar** — culminating in the August 2023 closure of the Hope Field Hospital CEmONC facility — while the Mae La system's institutional resilience and diversified funding base prevented equivalent documented disruption.

5. **Achieving ANC4+ coverage and facility delivery rates comparable to Mae La's has not been sufficient to close the mortality gap in Cox's Bazar.** The quality of intrapartum care — specifically midwife competence, EmONC readiness, blood transfusion access, and timely referral — represents the critical unclosed gap, one that the parallel humanitarian structure has proven structurally unable to replicate at the standard achieved by SMRU's integrated model.
